result:
The air that morning carried the metallic taste of autumn, sharp enough to remind me that another year was peeling away like old paint. I had not meant to wake before the sun, but an unquiet dream—something about a staircase that folded back on itself like a Möbius strip—left me sitting upright, heart hammering as though I had climbed that impossible flight in the flesh. The apartment was still except for the low mechanical sigh of the refrigerator and the softer human sigh that escaped my own lungs. I pressed my feet to the cool hardwood, felt the slight give of old boards that remember every previous tenant’s tread, and decided not to fight the wakefulness. There are moments when the mind hands you a ticket to an unscheduled departure; you can either crumple it up and stay in bed, or you can dress in the dark and see where the bus is going.

So I went out into the pre-dawn city, the neighborhood still a black-and-white photograph with only the faintest wash of rose gathering in the east. The streetlights had not yet relinquished their authority, and each one owned a small circle of amber that looked, from a distance, like a chain of miniature suns somebody had lowered on cables. My breath rolled out ahead of me in pale scarves. Somewhere a single car sighed over wet asphalt, tires kissing the road with the patience of someone who has driven all night and no longer cares if he ever arrives. I thought: we are all, always, in transit; even standing still is just a slower passage between two infinities.

I walked without a destination, the way I used to when I was twenty and convinced that getting lost was the only honest way to learn a city’s secrets. In those days I carried a pocket notebook and chewed on the ends of pens until plastic splinters decorated my lips like tiny translucent flags. I believed that if I wrote down enough fragments— overheard phrases, the shapes of cracks in stucco, the smell of limes rotting beneath a bodega awning— the city would eventually consent to be understood. I did not yet know that understanding is not a handshake but a slow osmosis, and that the city keeps changing faster than your inventory can refresh. Still, the old muscle memory asserted itself: I noticed how the new bagel shop had appropriated the brick wall of its neighbor for a trompe-l’oeil mural of a 1940s subway car, how someone had stuck a postage stamp of orange graffiti exactly in the center of the painted conductor’s forehead, giving him a third eye that looked amused and slightly stoned.

At the river I stopped. The water was a dark mirror, faithful enough to hold the skyline but treacherous enough to tilt it. A lone sculler slid past, oars dipping with the quiet regularity of a metronome. I watched the rower’s back recede and wondered what ledger he kept inside his skull—what debts owed, what collectibles of regret or tenderness. We never learn the stories of people who pass us once and vanish; they remain bright blank pages, and maybe that is mercy. Imagine if every silhouette demanded a chapter—we would drown in biographies before lunch.

Instead of turning home, I followed a pedestrian path that cut beneath the highway overpass. There the city’s soundtrack changed key: traffic overhead became a distant oceanic roar, and the air smelled of diesel trapped by concrete decades earlier. Someone had built a makeshift shrine against a piling: a plastic Virgin Mary, half-melted candles, a single child’s tennis shoe painted gold. I thought of the phrase “holy ground” and how we slap that label onto any acre where pain has seeped into the cracks like rainwater.

Farther along, I found a bench painted safety-orange, the kind cities use to claim public space back from chaos. Someone had carved "J+L 4ever" into the armrest. The word forever had been crossed out and below it someone else had scratched "for now." I sat there a long time thinking about that edit, how it was both a surrender and a mercy. We tell each other stories of permanence because the alternative feels like free fall, but maybe "for now" is the more generous promise. It says: I will stay until staying becomes a lie. It says: I will hold you with open hands.

A runner passed, earbuds in, feet hitting the ground in perfect meter. Her breath came out in little white puffs that dissolved instantly. I wondered what she listened to—maybe a true-crime podcast, maybe a playlist titled something like "Rage 4 Mile 3." I wondered if she felt the same odd immortality that sometimes visits in the middle of physical exertion, when the heart says oh, this is what I'm for, this pumping, this fire. I wanted to ask her, but she was already a hundred yards away, a bright green blur against the gray.

By the time the sun finally pulled itself over the horizon, I had walked all the way to the old rail yard. The city had turned part of it into a park, leaving stretches of track embedded in the grass like fossils. A interpretive sign explained that seventy years ago men with names like Dominic and Gus had stood here feeding coal into beasts made of iron. Now mothers pushed strollers over the same ground, and an apricot-colored dog was peeing on the sign. Progress smells like ammonia and grass, I thought, and almost laughed out loud.

I remembered a line from a forgotten poem: the present is always ruins in reverse. Maybe that's why I like mornings; they let you watch the wreckage knit itself back into buildings, watch the day assemble its costumes. We think of time as an arrow but it behaves more like a taffy pull—stretchy, sticky, prone to collapsing back onto itself. How else to explain the way an old song can suddenly occupy your throat thirty years after you last heard it, or the way the smell of crayons can haul you into the first grade in one instant?

My phone buzzed. A message from a friend I hadn't seen since before the pandemic: "Coffee later? I have news." I stared at the letters, feeling their weight. Simple words, complicated life. I typed yes, of course, then deleted it. Too brisk. I tried again: "I'd love that. Tell me when." Better. Warmer. I hit send and wondered what news qualifies as good anymore. I wanted to ask but thought better of it; some things need the cushioning of presence. I pocketed the phone and kept walking.

Another buzz. A photo of her cat sprawled on a windowsill, belly up, all trust and whiskers. "He misses you," she captioned. I smiled, surprised at how easily the expression came. Animals know nothing of quarantines or variants; they only know absence and return. I sent back a voice memo: "Tell him I miss him too. And you." My voice cracked a little on the last word. I hoped she wouldn't notice, or would pretend not to.

I passed the bakery. The owner, Mrs. Chen, was flipping the sign to OPEN. She saw me, waved through the glass. I waved back, hesitated, then went in. The bell above the door chimed like always. "The usual?" she asked. I nodded. While she bagged my sesame ball, she told me her daughter got into college away from the city. "Proud and scared," she said. I paid, left a tip big enough to feel meaningful but not extravagant. Outside, I bit into the warm pastry, sesame and sugar flooding my mouth. Simple geometries of softness and crunch. A pigeon eyed me, hopeful. I tore off a piece, tossed it. The bird pecked, then flew away, crumbs falling like tiny wishes.

I kept walking, eating, thinking about ruins in reverse, about how we rebuild ourselves daily from scraps. The river appeared, sluggish and bright. I stopped, leaned on the railing. Across the water, the skyline looked like a sentence written in glass and steel. I tried to read it, got only fragments: mortgage, deadline, lover, maybe. A barge slid underneath, low and slow, carrying gravel or garbage or both. The city keeps its secrets in plain sight. I finished the sesame ball, licked sugar from my fingers, turned back toward home. The day was a doorway now, wide open, and I had nowhere urgent to be except forward.

At a crosswalk I waited with an elderly man walking his ancient beagle. The dog sniffed my ankle, approved, sat on my foot. "He likes you," the man said, voice hoarse from years of cigarettes. "Likes everybody," I answered, but the old man shook his head. "No, he's picky. Real picky." The light changed; the dog trotted off my foot, tail ticking like a slow metronome. I watched them cross, taillights painting the snow ahead in crimson warnings. The beagle's ears flapped like broken umbrellas. I followed at a distance, listening to the engine purr, feeling the seat's worn fabric beneath my thighs. The city breathed around me, slow and deliberate, like a woman dressing in the dark. I turned left at the corner, tires whispering against packed snow. The plows hadn't reached this street yet; it lay pristine, untouched except for a single set of footprints leading to a doorway. I parked, killed the engine, sat in sudden silence. Snowflakes landed on the windshield, melted, landed again. I counted them until I lost track, then stepped out into the hush. My boots sank an inch, two. I walked to the building, key already in hand, but paused at the threshold. Something in the air—sharp, metallic, alive. I looked back at my tracks, already filling with new snow. Tomorrow they'd be gone, like tonight had never happened. I pushed inside, stomped the slush from my boots, climbed the stairs. At my door I hesitated again, key halfway to the lock. No sound from inside. I turned the key, stepped in, closed the door behind me. The apartment smelled of coffee and something else—familiar, comforting. I shed my coat, kicked off my boots, walked barefoot across cold tile to the kitchen. There, on the counter, a note in hurried script: "Couldn't wait. See you at the rink." I smiled despite myself, crumpled the note, tossed it toward the trash. Missed. Left it there. I poured coffee into a travel mug, added cream until it matched the color of his favorite sweater. Steam fogged my glasses; I wiped them on my shirt, took a sip. Too hot. I carried it to the window, watched snow erase the city block by block. Somewhere out there, he was lacing skates, humming off-key, waiting. I had time. I always had time, until I didn't. I finished the coffee, burning my tongue, not caring. Grabbed my keys, my coat, my scarf. At the door I paused, hand on the knob. Took a breath. Another. Then stepped out, ready to be ruined and rebuilt, again and again, on ice this time, under fluorescent sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
